***Project title: Project F.R.A.M.E.***   
***(Federated Ratcheting Architecture for Messaging Encryption)***

## 

## **1-Introduction**

Our project aims to develop a secure messaging system to ensure the users’ data confidentiality and authenticity during the communication. End-to-End systems encryption protects message content, yet recent research papers showed that security practices usually undermine some weaknesses. Those weaknesses can either be in the authenticity, session management, or surrounding infrastructure handling sensitive metadata or user identifier data.

The system design aims to ensure an end-to-end security stack. The stack is to ensure a robust key distribution and minimization of the service side trust. Moreover, the system is to ensure on the infrastructure level and deployment a hardened handling for meta data ensuring no leakage of them nor the personal identifiers as well as refined secrets management and configurations control. Lastly, amongst the set of challenges in such a problem is the different devices and instances synchronisation which our system would aim to handle ensuring resilient behavior from clients. 

## **2-Literature Review**

The architecture of secure messaging has evolved from a narrow focus on End-to-End Encryption (E2EE) to a multi-layered distributed systems problem. To guarantee confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity, security controls must exist throughout the entire lifecycle of the message being sent: client-side key creation and verification, backend cryptographic state management (with post-compromise security), and hardened implementation within a cloud-native infrastructure. Because of this, secure messaging should be treated as an integrated system and not as an independent cryptographic protocol.

This research was conducted from 2021 to 2025 across several premier venues such as USENIX Security, ACM CCS, NDSS, CRYPTO and IEEE journals indicate a central theme: formal cryptographic guarantees by themselves do not provide security in practice. Even mathematically sound protocols can have usability issues in their authentication ceremonies, metadata can leak via third-party services and misconfigured infrastructure in containerized environments. Therefore, secure messaging must be assessed through three lenses: backend cryptography architecture, user/consumer security and usability at the front-end client level, and hardened DevOps-implemented infrastructure.

The purpose of this literature review is to synthesize the advancements made within these three pillars, to identify any heralding operational/theoretical gaps, and to create a foundation for our proposed secure messaging system which concentrates primarily on end-to-end security assurance, user-centered cryptographic design and resilient governance of infrastructure.

1. ### **Backend (Server‑Side) Security**

The backend infrastructure that provides the controls of the E2EE system may also contain a single point of failure that is susceptible to attacks on the backend by third parties, such as through the distribution of illegitimate public keys or routing of asynchronous messages. Recent studies indicate that attacks against an E2EE backend may be possible due to the use of compromised or malicious backends used by E2EE service providers to perform man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks on all individual users and services being utilized. In addition, emerging legal definitions will likely require the use of third-party services for interoperability between centralized "gatekeeper" platforms in the future, which will impact the security of current backend infrastructures.

1. #### **Trust Minimization in Key Management**

End-to-end encryption (E2EE) messaging is vulnerable due to reliance on third-party servers as key distributors and certificates using public key infrastructure (PKI). As indicated by the recent Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on authentication ceremonies \[1\], in a typical application, an end-user must manually verify their keys out-of-band to protect against man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks. In most cases, the end-user does not do this which makes it very attractive for an attacker to compromise this backend server.

To address this issue, Chase et al., established SEEMless (Secure End-to-End Encrypted Messaging with less Trust) \[2\]. This system replaces the trusted key distributor server architecture with a clear and transparent logging system for public keys. This is accomplished by utilizing an append-only cryptographic dictionary to force the backend server to provide proof that the given public keys are the same for all users. If a backend server tries to distribute a false key to enable an MITM attack, this will be clearly proven mathematically due to the inconsistency of the key. While SEEMless succeeds in reducing trust in the backend server, it does assume that there is one trusted backend service and, therefore, additional questions arise regarding how these transparent logs would work with a distributed or federated backend. 

2. #### **Protocol State and Session Handling**

Backend servers are necessary to keep track of protocol state, especially in cases where the user maintains numerous simultaneous sessions across several devices. As detailed by Cremers et al. \[3\], they performed a formal analysis of session management within secure messaging systems that focused on evaluating the security characteristics of the Sesame layer of the Signal protocol. Their work demonstrated that while the Double Ratchet protocol provides forward secrecy (FS) and post-compromise security (PCS) at the per-session level, the way that the backend manages multiple sessions concurrently, at the conversation level, results in the compromising of FS and PCS guarantees. As such, clone attackers may exploit the backend session state to remain undetected in a conversation by taking advantage of the healing properties of the cryptographic ratchet. The results of this research indicate that the backend logic responsible for merging and routing asynchronous multi-device sessions must undergo extensive formal verification, as the cryptographic primitives used in the system cannot protect against the negative impact of inadequate backend parameter management.

3. #### **Metadata Protection Architectures**

Traditional backends can track the network and duration of any given connection, even with the highest level of end-to-end encryption and “perfect” key distribution. Even though it may seem that an E2EE chat app is offering complete privacy, as Sasy's System of Knowledge (SoK) on Metadata-Protecting Communications Systems (MPCS) \[4\] provides, backend servers possess significant amounts of metadata regarding communications between users that they can manipulate.

Sasy's SoK includes a thorough taxonomy of back-end architectures created to obscure routing information in the communication process of a user. The SoK provides detailed comparisons of various types of architectures, such as mixnets with Loopix vs. systems with private-read/write functions like Sabre and Talek. Sasy's SoK demonstrates that there is an inherent "scalability trilemma" with regard to backend architecture where it is practically impossible to achieve all three of the following: (i) low latency; (ii) horizontal scalability, and (iii) asynchronous delivery of communication from one user to another.

To this end, even though contemporary MPCS implementations substantially reduce the amount of metadata that can be collected by backend servers, they also impose tremendous computational overhead to perform this. This overhead makes it virtually impossible to implement systems with billions of users using contemporary architectures.

4. #### **Scalability and Group Architectures**

Backend operations become increasingly complex when utilizing Group Chat based messaging applications. Amir et al. have studied different models for backing the security of group communication using Secure Spread \[5\]. They highlight two major architectural trade-offs: integrated and layered. In an integrated model the backend will perform client-like functions such as supporting group membership, controlling access to the data, and managing key distribution; while in a layered model the backend is limited to being a simple relay of data and all cryptography end-to-end is performed by the clients. Their research findings indicate that; compared to a layered backend model, integrated backends provide greater scalability and resilience for large groups but also require greater levels of trust in the server infrastructure  to be used for messaging applications.

5. #### **Emerging Challenges: Cross-Backend Interoperability**

According to the article Len et al. \[6\], the European Union's Digital Markets Act (DMA) represents a major shift in how E2EE messaging gatekeepers like Whatsapp will be required to provide interoperability with third-party providers through their backends. Interoperability requires that separate backend infrastructures work together on identity resolution, protocol translation, and abuse prevention without a central authority acting as a mediator. Furthermore, when messages are transferred from one backend to another, they lose their standard E2EE guarantees. This implies that in order to support E2EE interoperability, backends need to create standardized, decentralized directory services and create protocols for establishing cross-platform connections without diminishing the security posture of the original gatekeeper platform.

The evolution of the backend from a trusted, centralized relay to a verifiable, metadata-resistant, and interoperable network in E2EE is developing rapidly. There are two major driving forces behind this change: academic scrutiny and regulatory requirements. The evolution of backend architectures will result in improved key transparency, better protection of metadata, and more interoperable and federated communication systems. Systems developed in the future will provide strong security guarantees throughout the lifecycle of a conversation, regardless of who provides the underlying services.

2. ### **Client‑Side (Frontend) Security \[7,11\]**

End-to-end encrypted (E2EE) messaging systems are designed to ensure that messages remain confidential between the sender and the recipient, without exposure to intermediaries ,Messages stay private from start to finish in systems using end-to-end encryption, visible only to those who send and receive them. Intermediary nodes never see the content, even when passing data along. Decentralized setups like federated networks lack a single authority responsible for handling keys or checking message integrity. Because of this absence, securing what happens on users’ devices becomes harder. Making sure encryption keys match correctly matters greatly. Leaks can happen at the app level if care isn’t taken during design. Client software must avoid exposing sensitive information through its interface. Cryptographic functions need protection against manipulation triggered by inputs appearing harmless. Attacks may arrive disguised as normal interactions with the screen or files already present. Security weakens unless every step resists tampering locally.

1. #### **Key Checks and Clear Information**

One of the main challenges on the edge of a decentralized, end-to-end encrypted system is making sure that individuals are actually using the right encryption keys when interacting with one another. Rather than a centralized system, a number of real-world systems attempt to address this by making key logs visible, such as Parakeet’s approach, to prevent a malicious actor from changing a valid key without being detected. This provides users a way to, on their own, ensure that a contact’s public key is correctly represented across the system to all other users. The visibility of this information also makes it more difficult for a malicious actor to tamper with this information within the client applications themselves.﻿  
Another potential solution is to utilize key-fingerprint verification, minimizing human error entirely. As this is represented on a user’s application screen, this is a small but important aspect of identity verification to ensure that users are able to safely and confidently interact with one another.

2. #### **Privacy Risks in Messaging Through App Exposures**

Despite the encryption of the data transfer path, the messenger program itself may inadvertently disclose small amounts of personal information. The research conducted by Samarin and his team shows that most popular chat programs send personal information to external alert systems, revealing vulnerabilities that cannot be solved through encryption methods. For any developer of decentralized messaging programs, this is a reminder to be aware of pop-up previews and background updates, which can inadvertently disclose hidden information. The information is encrypted and safe from prying eyes, but the way it looks on the screen can compromise all of this in an instant.

#### **3\.  Multi-Device Synchronization**

The source of front-end security is the various types of devices available today, and as such, security relies to a large extent on the cryptographic keys being correctly aligned among all these various devices. The research that Albrecht and others have conducted has looked at group chatting, using WhatsApp, between a variety of different mobile phones and has identified the need for continued management of cryptographic keys by clients as well as an uninterrupted flow of information across devices. What makes this increasingly complicated when the devices do not have any centralized authority is that they are all directly connected to each of the distributed nodes, which makes utilizing strong front-end encryption particularly important to preventing any interference or intrusion into the data being transmitted.

#### **4\. Forward and Future Secrecy**

Cryptographic ratchets, such as Olm, are key rotation mechanisms that rely on the UI code for handling state changes. If the frontend has UI errors, this could impair forwards secrecy. This was demonstrated in a study of encrypted messaging that described clear risks associated with forward ratcheting that failed without anyone being aware. It is also less well known that even the most complex encryption can be bypassed if attackers are able to get access to those messages because local execution errors occurred between the sender and receiver during the encryption process. Although the protocol will discuss how small coding errors could eliminate the security of new and old messages in general, it is equally important to understand the internal behaviour of clients as it relates to UI issues.

#### **5\. Effects on decentralized systems**

The study reveals that front-end security in decentralized, end-to-end encrypted messaging services is multi-layered. Key management, preventing unintended information leakage, and device synchronization are important considerations, all working in conjunction with good ratchet protocol implementation. Without a central server, clients are responsible for most of the encryption themselves. As such, there is a need to ensure that users can effectively manage security without needing to know how to do so technically. Studies suggest that usability and security are of equal importance in the design process.

3. ### **DevOps and Infrastructure Security\[12,17\]**

As for Devops, recent research works reveal the growing security risks in CI/CD and DevOps environments with the increasing use of automation, cloud computing, and third-party components in software development pipelines. suggests a new security framework based on the concept of DevSecOps, which addresses the security risks in CI/CD environments. The researcher points out fragmented security practices, vulnerability detection at the end of the development pipeline, and lack of governance in CI/CD environments\[12\]. Another paper suggests that the conventional approach in DevOps development environments considers the security validation step at the end of the development life cycle. The shift left approach, suggests the use of automated validation for the security of the software at the beginning of the development life cycle. Although both research works emphasize the importance of continuous integration in software development, they do not analyze the operational aspects of the attack vectors in the development pipeline\[13\].

going deeper into the security vulnerabilities that exist in the interfaces of the control plane of a Kubernetes environment. It is clear that even if the workloads have been properly secured, vulnerabilities in the control plane can undermine the trust in a system. From a DevOps perspective, it is interesting to see how the CI/CD pipelines interact with a Kubernetes environment through service accounts and API tokens. This creates a critical trust boundary that is difficult to manage. Even though this paper provides valuable insights about infrastructure security, it is clear that it does not explain how the control plane can be incorporated with a CI/CD permission management process. This is a gap between the security enforcement in a Kubernetes environment and the overall process of DevSecOps \[14\].

A complete and comprehensive end-to-end transaction monitoring and risk feedback system is proposed, specifically designed for use in DevOps environments. The paper clearly explains and discusses the shortcomings of traditional systems, especially in terms of providing predictive risk analysis and feedback. To solve this problem, a mix of microservices architecture and machine learning is proposed in order to enhance anomaly detection. The proposed system is excellent in terms of performance analysis and predictive analysis. However, security is not explicitly considered in this approach, specifically in terms of security within CI/CD pipelines, trust boundaries of APIs, integrity of automation scripts, and credential management. This shows that a complete and comprehensive approach is needed that combines process monitoring with end-to-end security validation\[15\].

GitHub Advanced Security (GHAS) in terms of its potential for integrating automated code scanning, secret detection, and dependency analysis in both GitHub and Azure DevOps. The research shows that this improves both the perception of vulnerabilities and the application of governance. Again, however, this is tool-centric and does not deal with the trust boundaries that are present between different parts of a pipeline, or different APIs and infrastructure\[16\].

## **3-Proposed Security Solutions** 

We propose a federated, decentralized E2EE messaging system that allows users to communicate using multiple interoperable homeservers instead of relying on a single central server for routing messages. Each user creates an account with their chosen federated node, and the nodes form a federation that routes messages from the sender to the recipient, while ensuring the confidentiality of the message content during transmission between the sender and the recipient. 

The client performs all of the cryptographic functions, including creating the asymmetric key pair, encrypting and decrypting the message content, and providing cryptographic verification of the identity of users communicating with each other via the client. The back-end nodes perform store and forward delivery like forward messages to the recipient, federation routing, device/session management for the end-users communicating with each other via the messaging application, and provide controls to prevent/reduce the likelihood of abuse. An important goal of the system's security model is to create a system where the cryptographic security of the system is preserved by enforcing an end-to-end encryption security stack that is not compromised by backend trust models, client implementation deficiencies, or metadata/secrets leaking from the backend infrastructure to third parties.

At a high level, the system is organized into four main components:

* **Client Applications (Web/Mobile/Desktop)**  
  * Perform E2EE operations (key agreement, message encryption, ratcheting, MAC verification).  
  * Provide user-friendly authentication ceremonies (QR/fingerprint comparison) and secure local state storage.

* **Federated Messaging Nodes (Homeservers)**  
  * Relay encrypted payloads, manage queues for offline delivery, and run federation APIs.  
  * Maintain minimal necessary protocol state for multi-device routing and synchronization.

* **Trust-Minimization & Verification Services**  
  * A key transparency / public key consistency mechanism so the backend cannot silently substitute keys at scale.  
  * Auditable logs and proofs that clients can verify.

* **DevOps / Deployment & Monitoring Layer**  
  * CI/CD, secrets management, hardened runtime, node isolation, and continuous security validation to prevent “secure crypto on insecure infrastructure” failures

1. ### **Backend (Server‑Side) Security**

The backend will consist of a federated array of homeservers that will provide the networking  layer for the chat application. Each user account will be located on one of the user's chosen homeservers, so when two users reside on different homeservers, their homeservers will communicate with each other via the federation API in order to transmit encrypted message events. Thus, the primary function of the backend is message transport and coordination. Specifically, it takes the encrypted payloads sent by clients, temporarily stores them if the recipient is offline, then sends them to the correct destination. The backend will never decrypt message payloads; encryption and authentication will only occur at the client level, while the backend will ensure reliable delivery and message synchronization.

The back end of the application is made up of a few major components as described below: **An API Gateway/Application** Server provides the front end of the application with multiple endpoints such as login, device registration, message sending and receiving, timeline retrieval, and attachment upload \-note that attachments are stored, encrypted, as blobs and metadata storage is minimal. **A Message Queue** implements a store-and-forward delivery method for those messages that have been sent . Once the device goes back online, the device will be notified of all messages in its message queue using a sequenced identifier. **A Federation Service** will connect the various Servers together by authenticating who the peers are and how they will establish the rules of federation trust, as well as relay encrypted events between the home servers. Finally, there is a **Directory and Key Distribution Layer** to store in the database  user identifier lookups necessary for message routing , and to provide Public Key publishing associated with those Users for the purpose of establishing a session, while maintaining Client verification as the basis for trust in this networked application, as opposed to Server Verification.

The backend retains only what is necessary for persistence by storing user accounts, device registrations, rooms, immutable encrypted message events and device delivery states so that the client can perform local sync without the need for interpreting content on the server side. The server also stores device lists for each user and implements a fan-out of encrypted events across web, mobile and desktop devices—all with crypto states remaining on client-side. In order for reliable operation of federation, rate limiting, request validation, availability monitoring, logging with minimal privacy impact, and background workers for retry delivery, housekeeping of attachment uploads, and federation backfill are implemented—keeping the server focused on routing, storage, and synchronization without compromising end-to-end guarantees.

2. ### **Client‑Side (Frontend) Security** 

The proposed frontend security architecture for Project F.R.A.M.E. designates the client application as a principal trusted element in the overall federated end-to-end encrypted messaging system. This allows all cryptographic activities to take place locally in the client application, ensuring that there is no leakage of information to the federated homeservers. The proposed frontend will incorporate key management and verification features that will help users to verify the authenticity of communications using fingerprint verification or other automated verification means. Furthermore, a key transparency feature will also be incorporated to ensure that there are no changes to the user’s public keys in the overall federated network. This will ensure that there are no man-in-the-middle attacks. The proposed frontend will also ensure that there are no privacy and implementation vulnerabilities that extend beyond encrypting messages. For example, storage access will be limited to avoid inadvertent leakage of information. 

The proposed frontend will also ensure that confidential information in messages is not revealed in notifications. The proposed frontend will ensure that multi-device synchronization occurs in a secure manner by verifying user credentials upon connection of a new device. The proposed frontend will also ensure that the client application communicates with the federated homeservers via authenticated API requests and responses. The proposed frontend will also ensure that potential client-side vulnerabilities are addressed, including script injection attacks and unauthorized access to stored credentials. In summary, the proposed frontend security mechanisms ensure the confidentiality and integrity of the F.R.A.M.E. messaging architecture. As such, the proposed frontend plays a central role in ensuring that the overall security of the messaging application is upheld even in the presence of an untrusted backend infrastructure.

3. ### **DevOps and Infrastructure Security**

The literature surveyed shows that an integrated approach of DevSecOps is best applied in order to strengthen CI/CD security. The framework that is likely to emerge is a DevSecOps-inspired approach that focuses on ensuring that security is integrated into each and every phase of the CI/CD process. This is in addition to a shift left approach that ensures that security is considered in each and every phase of the CI/CD process.

By directly incorporating automated security tools into CI/CD pipelines, security is enhanced through code scanning, detection of secrets, and checking dependencies for vulnerabilities. This provides a multi-layered approach to security through a mix of initial validation, automated enforcement through the CI/CD pipelines, enhanced infrastructure, monitoring, and integrated security tools to provide a complete end-to-end approach to DevSecOps.

## **4-Risk/Threat Analysis**

A comprehensive risk and threat analysis is a prerequisite for a proper distributed messaging system, considering aspects of confidentiality, integrity, and availability for all layers of operation. The security risks for a distributed system may stem from application logic, application programming interfaces, credentials, infrastructure, and automated deployments, among others. With a federated communication system, there are also issues of trust boundaries for each independent node, hence a wider threat surface. This section highlights potential threats and vulnerabilities for development, deployment, and runtime environments, considering mitigation techniques to ensure proper protection against system exploitation, misuse, and disruption.

### **Backend (Server‑Side) Security**

Risks associated with the backend include the server's continuous control over routing, key discovery and protocol coordination despite being unable to read the content of messages. A compromised or malicious backend server might attempt to substitute a user's key by providing a fake public key and positioning themselves between a user and an attacker. This scenario is quite common since most users never manually verify the keys they are using. Therefore, a system is only as safe as the key verification process; it must be difficult to circumvent.

Another significant concern for systems with backends is the complexity of device state and interaction across multiple devices. The backend has to track physical devices, deliver messages to multiple devices, and provide support for resynchronisation and recovery operations. If any of the following are incorrectly implemented, it may reduce the level of forward secrecy and create opportunities to exploit persistent vulnerabilities, even if the underlying time-based ratchet algorithm is securely designed. Examples of this include the following: device registration/removal, session registration, message order and handling of message replay ability.

Finally, metadata remains vulnerable even though end-to-end encryption has been applied. Patterns of communication will continue to be visible from the backend during the encrypted communications. Because excessive logging, long retention or durable communication graph storage can all lead to the unintentional disclosure of confidential materials, the parallel and federated nature of the service will increase potential exposure and vulnerability across multiple servers. Availability risks will still exist as endpoint federated entities can be targeted to disrupt delivery without ever having to decrypt the encrypted data.

### **Client‑Side (Frontend) Security** 

Security vulnerabilities of F.R.A.M.E. 's frontend generally come up when the process of identifying the participants fails without any central control. Instead of using one source, the process becomes vulnerable when rogue nodes intervene during communication. This is because a dishonest participant could add their own false public key while pretending to be another. This is similar to the common MitM attacks. Studies done on the security of the encrypted message show that people do not verify the digital fingerprint themselves. This leaves them vulnerable to attacks. Because of this, the process of checking automatically takes place, using information from earlier research done \[10\]. Ideas, like the ones used in Parakeet \[7\], come up with transparency logs. Each device will have information that will allow it to recognize any sudden change of ownership. This way, even when the encryption is based on good mathematical theory, the information displayed will not betray that unknowingly.

Not stopping at intercepted messages, the design also grapples with unintended metadata flows through indirect paths \- especially those tied to operating system functions. Studies examining current chat platforms \[8\] reveal flaws allowing private details like who sent a message or snippets of content to escape toward outside push systems such as Firebase or Apple’s APNs, long before any user device unlocks them. Given the goal here \- complete confidentiality \- the interface cannot assume these alerts are safe. To respond, notification triggers on the device will stay blank except for waking internal processes; actual reading happens locally only after launch, cutting raw data off from external signaling networks \[8\].

Finally, managing messages in sync over a number of devices also creates new threats related to state inconsistencies. Studies on the design of WhatsApp have demonstrated just how complex managing synchronized encryption states over a number of devices can be, allowing attackers to introduce fake devices during active sessions. Frontend management of devices in decentralized systems handles connection management between devices on its own. To overcome these vulnerabilities, our approach utilizes local state management using the Olm protocol to ensure that each authenticated device has its own unique ratchet path that matches precisely with other devices; every added device is authenticated in real-time using the existing secure interface. This way, attackers are unable to introduce fake devices that can connect to old or upcoming messages.

### **DevOps and Infrastructure Security**

In most present-day federated message systems implemented within a CI/CD-driven ecosystem, threats exist at all stages: the pipeline, infrastructure, and API surface. For instance, the CI/CD process itself is a major target because it is granted broad permissions to execute automated deployments. If a hacker infiltrates a CI/CD process, they can put malicious code, exfiltrate secrets, or introduce unauthorized configuration changes. Similarly, a federated message system implemented with poor secrets management practices such as hardcoding secrets within code, can also lead to a hacker masking themselves as a service or accessing backend systems. API endpoints for message passing, authentication, and federation also introduce various risks: replay attacks, flood attempts, and unauthorized access especially when implemented within a distributed environment running on a managed platform.

To mitigate these risks, we use a layered approach to security with DevSecOps. Validation occurs early in the build process through automated static analysis, dependency checking, and secret detection on every code change. We protect the process with branch protection, least privilege permissions on the workflow, and deployment approvals to limit pipeline abuse. We also protect secrets with encrypted environment variables with controlled access and prefer short-lived credentials to limit potential secret exposure. For infrastructure security, we limit permissions during runtime, keep environments siloed, and strictly manage admin interfaces. We also protect API endpoints through enforcement of authentication, rate limiting, and replay guard implementations.

Continuous monitoring is used in conjunction with the above measures to detect unusual traffic, failed login attempts, and suspicious federation activities. Feedback loops are also sent back to the development process, making it easier to make quick fixes. This security solution, by incorporating security into the development, CI/CD automation, deployment, and monitoring, reduces the risk of tampering with the CI/CD pipeline, leakage of credentials, misuse of the API, and denial of service attacks, while still maintaining the speed of CD.

## **5-Tools and Technologies**

The implementation of the system relies on the selection of appropriate tools and technology to ensure scalability, security, and ease of maintenance both during development and deployment. The tools and technology stack must support source control, automated integration/deployment, containerization, secure configuration management, runtime hosting, and monitoring. With these tools integrated into a single system, we are able to achieve continuous delivery, a standardized build environment, and integrated security validation to lay a foundation for a distributed system to operate securely.

Below is a a summary image (generated with Nano-banana based on our tech stack for visualization purposes) of our tech stack followed by a detailed breakdown of the tech stack and tools of each pillar of our system and their role:

![][image1]

### **Backend (Server‑Side) Security**

| Technology | Role |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Node.js (Express.js)** | Federated messaging server and REST APIs |
| **PostgreSQL** | Secure storage for users, device sessions, and metadata |
| **Redis** | Message queue and temporary session storage |
| **JWT / OAuth2** | Secure authentication and API authorization |
| **TLS (HTTPS)** | Encrypted communication between nodes |
| **libolm (Server Verification)** | Verification of encrypted sessions |
| **Key Transparency Logs (Merkle Tree)** | Public key consistency verification |
| **Rate Limiting (Express Middleware)** | Prevent API abuse and flooding attacks |
| **Federation APIs** | Communication between homeservers |

### **Client‑Side (Frontend) Security** 

| Technology | Role |
| ----- | ----- |
| TypeScript / JavaScript | Implements the client-side messaging application and cryptographic logic |
| libolm | Provides Olm and Megolm ratchet protocols for End-to-End Encryption |
| Web Crypto API | Generates cryptographic keys and performs secure encryption operations in the browser |
| IndexedDB | Stores encrypted messages and cryptographic keys securely on the user device |
| Service Workers | Handle background message processing and secure notification delivery |
| DOMPurify | Sanitizes incoming messages to prevent Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks |
| HTTPS (TLS) | Secures communication between the client and federated homeservers |
| Fetch API / REST Client | Enables authenticated communication with backend federation APIs |

### **DevOps and Infrastructure Security**

The project leverages GitHub as the central location for source control and collaboration. It also implements features like protected branches, reviews in pull requests, and strict merge policies. GitHub Actions are leveraged as the CI/CD engine to automate builds, tests, security scanning, and deployments. To ensure that security is at the forefront at all times, GitHub Advanced Security is integrated into the pipeline to offer automated static code scanning, vulnerability scanning, and secrets scanning, which all happen before any release.

We will containerize our application using Docker to ensure that builds are reproducible from development to production. The infrastructure provisioning and environment setup will be managed directly from our CI/CD pipeline using Railway, which reduces the number of steps in our pipeline. We will ensure that secrets and other sensitive information are properly secured by storing environment variables and secrets in GitHub Secrets and the deployment platform’s config store, adhering to the principle of least privilege.

Moreover, operational visibility will be maintained through the use of centralized logging and monitoring tools, which will be integrated in the deployment environment, thereby ensuring the monitoring of the application’s performance, detection of anomalies, and the ability to roll back the application in case of issues. These technologies, in essence, form an automated, secure, and validated delivery pipeline, which is in line with the DevOps approach of scalability, reliability, and release management.

| Technology | Role |
| ----- | ----- |
| GitHub | Version control |
| GitHub Actions | CI/CD automation |
| GitHub Advanced Security | Code scanning & secret detection |
| Docker | Containerization |
| Railway | Deployment platform |
| GitHub Secrets | Secure credential storage |
| Logging Tools | Monitoring and anomaly detection |

## **6-Timeline and Task Schedule**

| Week | Phase | Major milestone | Ahmed (Backend) | Mohamed (Frontend) | Hossam (DevOps/Infra) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 1 | Planning and setup | Scope and architecture locked, repo and environments ready | Finalize backend modules, data model, API contracts | Define UX flows, client requirements, API contract mapping | Repo governance, CI skeleton, Docker baseline, env strategy |
| 2 | Core skeletons | Local system skeleton running, client can authenticate and call APIs | Implement auth, device registration, room creation, basic send and fetch timeline, DB schema | Build client skeleton, auth flow, basic chat UI wired to APIs | Containerize backend and DB, add CI checks, basic staging workflow |
| 3 | E2EE message lifecycle | Encrypted send and receive works on same server | Add store-and-forward queue, delivery state tracking, sequencing, attachment path | Integrate crypto library, key/session setup, encrypt/decrypt, secure local storage | Enable TLS in dev/staging, baseline logging and monitoring hooks |
| 4 | Federation | Cross-server delivery working in staging | Implement federation relay, peer auth, routing lookup, federation rules | Support remote users UX, cross-server messaging flows | Deploy two-node federation, add rate limiting and request validation |
| 5 | Verification and key handling | Key publishing and client verification visible | Implement key publishing endpoints, transparency log and proofs API | Add verification UX, key change alerts, basic proof checks | Secrets management, rotation in staging, privacy-minimized logging |
| 6 | Multi-device and hardening | Multi-device fan-out stable, hardening pass done | Device fan-out, resync endpoints, replay/ordering checks, minimal metadata retention | Device linking flow, frontend hardening, error handling | Alerts for spikes and failures, container hardening checks, policy gates |
| 7 | Testing and documentation | Test evidence \+ demo script ready | Backend security test cases, API and DB documentation | Client security test cases, UX verification tests, demo walkthrough | CI/CD evidence, deployment reproducibility tests, infra diagram |
| 8 | Final integration and delivery | Stable demo, final report and presentation submitted | Integration fixes, performance sanity checks, finalize backend sections | UI polish, finalize client write-up, demo rehearsal | Final deployment, monitoring dashboards, final infra write-up |

## **7-References**

\[1\] J. Niemantsverdriet et al., "SoK: An Analysis of End-to-End Encryption and Authentication Ceremonies in Secure Messaging Systems," in *Proceedings of the 16th ACM Conference on Security and Privacy in Wireless and Mobile Networks (WiSec '23)*, 2023\.

\[2\] M. Chase, A. Deshpande, E. Ghosh, and H. Malvai, "SEEMless: Secure End-to-End Encrypted Messaging with less Trust," in *Proceedings of the 2019 ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security (CCS '19)*, 2019, pp. 1639–1656.

\[3\] C. Cremers, C. Jacomme, and A. Naska, "Formal Analysis of Session-Handling in Secure Messaging: Lifting Security from Sessions to Conversations," in *Proceedings of the 32nd USENIX Security Symposium*, 2023, pp. 1235–1252.

\[4\] S. Sasy and I. Goldberg, "SoK: Metadata-Protecting Communication Systems," *Proceedings on Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PoPETs)*, vol. 2024, no. 1, pp. 509–524, 2024\.

\[5\] Y. Amir, C. Nita-Rotaru, J. Stanton, and G. Tsudik, "Secure Spread: An Integrated Architecture for Secure Group Communication," *IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing*, vol. 2, no. 3, pp. 248-261, July-Sept. 2005\.

\[6\] J. Len, E. Ghosh, P. Grubbs, and P. Rösler, "Interoperability in End-to-End Encrypted Messaging," *ACM Symposium on Computer Science and Law (CS\&Law)*, to appear 2026\.

\[7\] Parakeet: Practical key transparency for end-to-end encrypted messaging, https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/081.pdf (accessed Feb. 26, 2026).

\[8\] N. Samarin et al., “The medium is the message: How secure messaging apps leak sensitive data to push notification services,” Proceedings on Privacy Enhancing Technologies, https://petsymposium.org/popets/2024/popets-2024-0151.php (accessed Feb. 26, 2026).

\[9\] M. R. Albrecht, B. Dowling, and D. Jones, “Formal analysis of multi-device group messaging in WhatsApp,” IACR Cryptology ePrint Archive, https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/794 (accessed Feb. 26, 2026).

\[10\] Sok: An analysis of end-to-end encryption and authentication, https://nsaxena.engr.tamu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/238/2023/10/3558482.3581773.pdf (accessed Feb. 26, 2026).

\[11\] A. K. Gaurav et al., “Automating Key Fingerprinting in End-to-End Encrypted Messaging,” 2025\. Available: https://nsaxena.engr.tamu.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/238/2025/08/automating-key-fingerprinting.pdf (accessed Feb. 26, 2026).

\[12\] A. Mishra, “DevSecOps-driven security framework for CI/CD pipeline risk mitigation,” International Journal of Computing and Engineering, vol. 7, no. 18, pp. 12–23, Jul. 2025\. doi:10.47941/ijce.3047

\[13\] Bhardwaj, Avdhesh Kumar. (2026). Integrating Security Early A Shift-Left Model for DevSecOps in Modern Software Pipelines.

\[14\] C. Wang et al., “Losing control: Exposing security weaknesses of Kubernetes Control Plane interfaces,” Computers \&amp; Security, vol. 160, p. 104704, Jan. 2026\. doi:10.1016/j.cose.2025.104704

\[15\] Z. Liu, “Design of a full-process transaction monitoring and risk feedback system for DevOps based on microservices architecture and Machine Learning Methods,” Procedia Computer Science, vol. 262, pp. 948–954, 2025\. doi:10.1016/j.procs.2025.05.129 

\[16\] V. Manolov, D. Gotseva, and N. Hinov, “Analysis of github advanced security: Security integration in GitHub and azure devops,” Future Internet, vol. 18, no. 2, p. 99, Feb. 2026\. doi:10.3390/fi18020099
